title: Low-cost American airline goes bust
text:
Scores of passengers are stranded and thousands of workers have been stood down after American low-cost carrier Spirit Airlines shut down.

The budget airline announced it would start an "orderly wind-down of operations, effective immediately" on May 2 after 34 years in the business.

"All Spirit flights have been cancelled and Spirit guests should not go to the airport," the airline said in a statement.

An electronic check-in kiosk announces Spirit Airlines shutdown on Saturday, May 2, 2026 at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport in Atlanta. (AP Photo/Jeff Amy) (AP)

Passengers arrived at airports across the US this morning to empty check-in desks and signs informing them all flights had been cancelled and customer service was no longer available.

﻿Spirit cited the rising cost of jet fuel, which has more than doubled since the war in the Middle East broke out in February, alongside "other pressures on the business" for the closure, which will leave 17,000 employees without a job.

"In March 2026, we reached an agreement with our bondholders on a restructuring plan that would have allowed us to emerge as a go-forward business," Spirit President and Chief Executive Officer Dave Davis said.

"However, the sudden and sustained rise in fuel prices in recent weeks ultimately has left us with no alternative but to pursue an orderly wind-down of the Company."

Sustaining the business would have required hundreds of millions of dollars the airline "simply does not have and could not procure," Davis said.﻿

"This is tremendously disappointing and not the outcome any of us wanted," he said.

Spirit's bankruptcy has sparked concerns other low-cost carriers could be next.

An electronic check-in kiosk announces Spirit Airlines shutdown on Saturday, May 2, 2026 at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport in Atlanta. (AP Photo/Jeff Amy) (AP)

But the airline faced significant challenges ﻿before the conflict broke out, including large debt, two bankruptcies within a year and a failed government bailout bid just weeks ago.

After failing to bounce back from the COVID-19 pandemic, the airline filed for bankruptcy in November 2024, having haemorrhaged more than US$2.5 billion ($3.5 billion) since the start of 2020.

Spirit sought bankruptcy protection for the second time in August 2025, reporting $11.2 billion in debts and $11.9 billion in assets.

A major restructure followed, which sought to cut flights and operational costs by furloughing and downgrading pilots﻿ and flight attendants.

Spirit told creditors the restructure would allow them to post a net $304 million profit by 2027.

But just six months later, on April 18, the airline asked the US government for an emergency bailout.

A traveler walks past covered Spirit Airlines kiosks, Saturday, May 2, 2026, at George Bush Intercontinental Airport, in Houston. (AP Photo/Lekan Oyekanmi) (AP)

Bail-out negotiations with the Trump Administration, reported to be worth $500 million, collapsed﻿ in recent days, sparking the airline's collapse today.

﻿The airline serviced destinations across the US, Central America, the Caribbean and South America.

Around 9000 flights were scheduled until the end of May, according to Cirium analytics, potentially impacting millions of passengers across the month.

﻿Refunds will be processed for impacted customers who booked directly with the airline, Spirit said, while passengers with third-party bookings should contact their travel agent.

"Compensation for Guests who booked flights using any other methods, including a voucher, credit or Free Spirit points, will be determined at a later date," Spirit said.

title: How Pokémon cards are becoming a survival strategy for Aussie retailers
text:
The surging price ﻿of Pokémon products has been no secret over the past few years, with some ﻿turning the trading cards into a lucrative side hustle .

And as the ﻿cards become a commodity, larger businesses are now following suit.

George Kozma started the multi-million-dollar streetwear business, Waves Never Die, in 2018, out of his home in Sydney's west and has kept his finger on the pulse of upcoming trends ever since.

Waves Never Die began transformed into an entire shopping and dessert experience. (Supplied)

He began selling high-end sneakers and streetwear from brands such as Supreme and Yeezy before adding coveted collectibles like Labubus and even Rhode beauty products into the mix during their highest demand.

After transforming a Moorebank warehouse into a streetwear store and dessert shop, he now has his eyes set on Pokémon cards﻿, a hobby he claims is "going through the roof".

"I've got 45 to 50-year-old men calling me. I had one guy ask me for 50 packs of the bundles.

"We're talking 50-year-old men who've transitioned from watches to Pokémon, because Pokémon holds more value than a Rolex.﻿"

"You buy a Rolex, you're gonna flip it maybe in a year and make [up to] five grand, while you buy 50 packs worth five grand, and you might pull out a card that's worth a few grand.

"It's got a gambling aspect, it's like a thrill.﻿"

George isn't wrong. Earlier this year, professional wrestler and YouTuber Logan Paul broke a world record after selling ﻿his Pikachu Illustrator card for an eye-watering $US16.5 million ($23.3 million) in an auction, a card many consider the rarest of all time.

Logan Paul sold the PSA 10 graded card for more than $23 million. (Goldin)

That is enough money to buy a harbourfront mansion in a luxury Sydney suburb, a garage full of supercars , or, in this instance, a piece of holographic cardboard cased in plastic and gold. ﻿

Australian YouTuber Michael Anderson, better known as Unlisted Leaf, garnered more than 2.7 million subscribers and almost 1 billion views by opening Pokémon packs and even had the opportunity to reveal the value of a card that Paul opened in a pack live on stream in 2021.

"One of the most incredible cards I've ever filmed was the PSA 10 1st Edition Charizard, the same card that Logan Paul just sold for over $1 million," he told Nine.com.au.

"I got to reveal the PSA 10 grade of that card with its owner Will years before that sale, the whole day was very surreal."

Unlisted Leaf (middle) holding the PSA 10 1st Edition Logan Paul Charizard. (Supplied)

The card went on to sell for more than $950,000. (Supplied)

Anderson told Nine.com.au he believes that ﻿ COVID lockdowns and influencers such as Paul promoting the trading cards created the "perfect storm" that skyrocketed collecting to new heights.

"I've seen card shows go from small events with maybe 100 attendees to massive conventions with thousands of people walking through the doors," he said.

That is the story for ﻿Trading Card Show Australia founders Drei and Blair, who say their event began in 2021 with 20 vendors at a small community ﻿hall in Sydney's Inner West before it became a national show.

Trading Card Show Australia now attracts thousands of vendors and collectors. (Supplied)

"Each show seems to be breaking records. We just did one in Brisbane with over 4500 people through the door," Blair said.

They say that Pokémon cards make up "at least 80 per cent" of tables at their shows, and they have seen cards worth as much as $80,000 at the events.﻿

"Retail stores will always have a place in the hobby, though, especially as a space for people just to go during the week, to buy cards and sell cards," Blair said.

Despite being one of the few secondary retailers flipping Pokémon products, George said it is only a matter of time before his competitors hop on the trend.

Some secondary stores have begun selling sealed Pokemon products for well over their retail price. (Supplied)

Rare cards can fetch well over hundreds of dollars. (Supplied)

"Everyone's gonna start, I guarantee every secondary resell store in Australia will be selling Pokémon within the next two, three, four weeks, because no one is spending money on anything else.

"Pokémon sells out instantly, they've got to go to the secondary market.﻿

"You just got to chase what's selling at the moment," George said.

"In these current times, it's so tough, so we're hustling harder than ever."

title: Scammers attempt to extort Sydney removalist with sudden flurry of negative reviews
text:
The owner of a Sydney removalist business has been targeted by scammers who tanked her online reviews in a bid to extort money from her.

A sudden flurry of negative reviews came as a shock for Move It With Gina owner and single mother-of-six Gina.

"I started it 11 years ago, packing and cleaning," she said.

READ MORE: Cops called in as petrol price plunges to unbelievable low ﻿

The negative reviews came as a shock for owner and single mum-of-six Gina. (9News)

"All my clients wanted a removalist so I ended up buying a truck.

"Four days ago we got one-star reviews in consecutive orders within every half an hour.

"When we had a look at the reviews and who they're from, we were like, 'we haven't serviced these people'."﻿

Gina started getting suspicious text messages. (9News)

Then Gina started getting suspicious text messages.

"I have received A order for posting thirty bad rewiews (sic) on your business. Now he is ignoring My message please pay my loss. 60$. Otherwise I'm going to More One Star review," one of the messages said.

Gina said she ignored the messages.

Cybersecurity expert Simon Smith said there were a few tell-tale signs of a fake review.

It could include generic language and a succession of negative reviews in a short period of time.

"The most important thing is to never pay the extortion," Smith said.

For Gina, it took days for the reviews to be taken down.

9News attempted to get a response from the scammer on their number based overseas but they have since gone quiet.

title: Capital city smashes 144-year-old heat record
text:
Deniliquin in the western Riverina region hit 28.5 degrees yesterday, nudging just above the old record of 28.0 degrees.

Hay ﻿also exceeded its May record, hitting 29.2 degrees, beating the old record of 29.1 by a whisker.

title: Thousands of Aussies could lose popular $22 billion tax break in just 10 days
text:
Australians are set to be hit by major tax changes, including losing access to a popular concession, in this year's federal budget , which is shaping up to be one of the most significant in decades. ﻿

Treasurer Jim Chalmers has pledged to deliver an "ambitious" set of national accounts on May 12 in the face of global conflict and rising inflation, while also saying the economy is being held "hostage" by the war in Iran.

The budget is set to contain three central packages — ﻿tax reform, spending savings, and productivity and investment — that are set to address the intergenerational inequity issues younger people are facing.

Here is ﻿what you can expect when Chalmers delivers his budget later this month.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers is about to unveil the federal budget. (Alex Ellinghausen)

Tax reform ﻿

The government has been dancing around exactly what tax reform it will hand down , but two of the measures that are at least on the table are changes to the $22 billion-a-year capital gains tax (CGT) discount and negative gearing.﻿

Critics have blamed the combination of the policies for contributing to the housing crisis, with a Greens-led Senate inquiry earlier this year finding the CGT discount is flawed and benefits investors over first-home buyers .

An Oxfam report earlier this year found nearly half the beneficiaries of the CGT discount were 24,000 of Australia's richest people .

The specific reforms, including whether grandfathering provisions will be included for investors who﻿ are using the discount and negative gearing, are still a bit of a mystery.

The government hasn't confirmed the changes, but has repeatedly failed to rule them out when given the chance, with Chalmers and Prime Minister Anthony Albanese ﻿instead saying the budget will tackle intergenerational unfairness in the tax system and housing market.

"What we are determined to see is a fairer economy that works for more people, including for younger people﻿," Chalmers said this week.

If reformed, it would be the first time the CGT discount is rolled back since the Howard government raised it to 50 per cent in 1999 and the first time negative gearing is ﻿changed since it was temporarily limited by the Hawke/Keating government in 1987. ﻿

In his pre-budget speech in March, Chalmers said the tax reform package would also make the system simpler and sustainable and drive business investment but only "if we can afford to". ﻿﻿

"How much of that we can do in May depends a bit on fiscal considerations, international developments, and also, of course, cabinet deliberations," he said at the time.

The government will attempt to address intergenerational inequity in the housing market in the budget. (Joe Armao)

NDIS cuts ﻿

Health Minister Mark Butler announced $15 billion would be cut from the ﻿National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) over the next four years to clamp down on ballooning costs and prevent it from becoming "an ATM for shonks, grifters, fraudsters and crooks".

Changes include tighter criteria, standardised and evidence-based assessments, fraud prevention and reduced spending on social and community participation per participant and daily activities.

Initial modelling shows the changes will reduce the number of people on the NDIS from 760,000 to 600,000 by the end of the decade. ﻿

"It costs too much and is growing too fast," Butler said in April.

"We can't afford for the NDIS to continue growing at its current rate."

READ MORE: ﻿ Man accused of killing Labor strategist to fight charge

The government has announced it will cut billions from the NDIS. (Getty)

﻿ Defence boost

Defence will receive an additional $53 billion over the next decade, increasing spending to 3 per cent of Australia's GDP by 2033 under the NATO methodology.

US President Donald Trump has been pressuring Australia and NATO countries to raise their defence spending, complaining that the US does a lot of the heavy lifting.

The funding boost, announced last month by Defence Minister Richard Marles, was partly paid for by the sale of military land and will deliver greater warfare capabilities, systems and communications.

The ADF is getting a $53 billion boost. (Australian Defence Force)

Cheaper fuel

The federal government halved the fuel excise and removed the Heavy Vehicle Road User Charge ﻿from April 1 to June 30 to provide some relief for motorists facing soaring fuel costs, which will be funded in the budget.

The measure was estimated to reduce fuel costs by ﻿26.3 cents per litre and almost $19 on a 65-litre tank.

The Australian ﻿Competition and Consumer Commission is monitoring fuel prices across the country to ensure the cost savings are being passed on.

More announcements ﻿

$1000 instant receipt-free tax deduction ﻿for work-related expenses for about 6.2 million workers.

Reducing the private health insurance rebate for those over 65 to the same level as everyone else.

﻿New Payday Super rules mandating employers pay superannuation at the same time as salary and wage payments will come into effect from July 1.

title: Residents of seaside Victorian town braced for Macca's battle
text:
R﻿esidents of a popular seaside town in Victoria are bracing for an uphill battle to block a McDonald's store from opening on the same street as a primary school.

Members of Phillip Island's tight-knit community have banded together to fight the fast food giant from being approved for development just few hundred metres from a primary school in the township of Cowes.

McDonald's has submitted plans to Bass Coast Shire Council to build a﻿ restaurant on 194 Settlement Road in Cowes, which has a population of around 6500 people but often balloons in size due to tourist numbers.

An artist's impression of a McDonald's in Cowes, Phillip Island. (Bass Coast Shire Council)

Do you have a story? Contact reporter April Glover at april.glover@nine.com.au

The permit was lodged on April 1 and it has attracted vocal opposition from thousands of locals.

Resident Travis Thompson has lived in the area since 2012 and told nine.com.au the proposed site of McDonald's is "not a great choice" for Cowes.

"The intersection they want to put it on is already a congested intersection. On any busy weekend, there's already traffic coming into Cowes down the main road," Thompson said.

"Adding a McDonald's there with the drive-through is only going to increase congestion, as well as any time at school start or end of school day, there's traffic there all the time, and that's going to be an issue."

Thompson said, if approved, it would be the only drive-through on Phillip Island.

He'﻿d be "pleasantly surprised" if the community outrage results in blocking the Macca's, but knows it would not be an easy fight.

"But McDonald's is huge... they have tonnes of money," he said.

His cautious optimism is shared by fellow long-time residents.

Resident Travis Thompson has lived in the area since 2012. (Supplied)

High school student stands up to Macca's﻿

An online petition opposing the McDonald's in Cowes has attracted over 5000 signatures.

It inspired a second, smaller petition launched by 17-year-old Cowes local Airlie Minter.

Minter ﻿fiercely opposes the McDonald's and said building it within walking distance from a primary school would be "atrocious" for the community.

"Even at my high school, after school finishes, people often say, 'Oh, let's go get something from Macca's. It's not good, it's unhealthy," Minter told nine.com.au.

"Especially for primary school kids, they are much more naive... they're children, an easy target [for fast food]."

Airlie Minter, 17, said she hopes the council will reject the McDonald's application. (Supplied)

The high school stud﻿ent has lived on Phillip Island her entire life and is passionate about protecting her local environment.

She warned that a McDonald's could threaten the region's natural beauty and result in a scourge of extra litter across the island.

"That happens as it is with all the fish and chip places, people leave rubbish everywhere, and with how much rubbish McDonald's produces every single order, it's just going to skyrocket," Minter added.

Minter and Thompson are among thousands of residents who are ﻿appealing to Bass Coast Shire Council to reject the application.

It's a tough task challenging a giant of industry like the Golden Arches.

Residents of Northcote in Melbourne's inner-north can attest to this after their petition to stop a McDonald's from opening on High Street was unsuccessful last month.

﻿"People told me, people I really care about, told me there is no point in trying to fight... it's not going to change anything," Minter said.

"And I said, Well, what if it does? What if this one thing that I've done changes everything?

"I'm really hopeful that maybe it could change the council's perspective."

Bass Coast Shire Council said it would assess the McDonald's application against the Bass Coast Planning Scheme.

"Council is aware this proposal will attract community interest, and teams will work with the community, local businesses, and relevant authorities before preparing a recommendation to Council," it said in a statement.

Minter warned that a McDonald's could result in a scourge of extra litter across the island. (Supplied)

McDonald's Australia said the Cowes location would create 100-120 full-time, part-time and casual jobs for the area and would be owned by a local franchisee.

"The restaurant would service both local residents and visitors to the area, offering great value, convenient service, and a welcoming place for people to visit," a spokesperson said.

"We're committed to being a good neighbour and making a positive social and economic contribution to Cowes."

Dan Murphy's proposal sparks 'big box' takeover fears﻿

Dan Murphy's has applied for a liquor licence at 137 Thompson Avenue in Cowes.

The addition of a chain liquor store nearby the proposed McDonald's site has prompted similar fears about the "big box" takeover of Phillip Island.

The liquor store would be built on a 703-square-metre trading area and sit on a commercial zone.

"I don't approve of the 'Walmartification'... I want local businesses here, people who have an investment in the community," Thompson said.

A public notice will be on display for community feedback until May 15.﻿

The Dan Murphy's proposal falls under the Victorian Commission for Gambling and Liquor Regulation, not the council.

In a statement, the parent company for Dan Murphy's said it ﻿"welcomes the opportunity to bring a Dan Murphy's store to Cowe".

"The application has been advertised and local stakeholders can provide feedback before 15th May 2026," the spokesperson said.

"Over the last six months, Endeavour has been, and continues to, engage with the Cowes community on the proposal."

title: Live: Ponga returns as Knights and Rabbitohs kick off NRL Sunday's triple header
text:
- with AAP

(Getty Images)

This match in the Hunter seems to be a case of a long-sought after reunion of sorts, with a whole host of previously injured players making their return to action.

However, that's not going to be the case for Brandon Smith, who will miss a month of NRL action with his latest calf injury in another frustrating blow for the luckless South Sydney forward.

Smith had been in excellent form since returning from his pre-season calf injury in round six, but re-injured himself in last week's hoodoo-busting defeat of Melbourne.

"He's pretty disappointed, to be honest with you, he's out for another month," Bennett said. "He loves to play and he brings a lot to the team. But we all have our disappointments so he's got to handle it."

At least Adam Elliott, a former Knight who has been sidelined since signing for Souths, could return today, joining the Bunnies' bench for his first game in nearly a year.

Elliott trained with the team on Saturday and is set to be promoted from Bennett's extended squad to the bench for his first game since last May.

A biceps injury curtailed Elliott's final season with the Knights before calf problems delayed his start to life at Souths, who offered him a one-year deal over the summer.

title: Government announces program to unlock land for oil infrastructure
text:
The state government has announced a plan to unlock state-owned land for potential oil storage and refineries near ports along the Queensland coast.

The plan will see land near ports in Brisbane, Townsville, Mackay, Gladstone, Abbot Point and Bundaberg made available for expressions of interest by industry for construction of oil infrastructure.

Successful tenders will be able to build fuel storage tanks on the land, with the option to also build refineries.

State-owned land along the coast will be opened for oil development, including near the Port of Brisbane. (ABC News: Charlie McLean)

The new Accelerating Fuel Infrastructure Program includes an audit of potential land for development, rapid market engagement for construction, and a streamlined process for approvals on new oil projects.

It's unclear when construction could start on projects.

The government will also accept proposals from industry on sites with potential for development.

Ampol's Lytton Refinery in Brisbane is one of only two active facilities in Australia. (AAP: Darren England)

Intended to improve 'sovereign capability'

The project is part of the government's Queensland Fuel Security Plan, which Premier David Crisafulli said was intended to deliver relief from rising fuel prices and provide "sovereign capability" to protect from future shocks.

"We said that we would commit to pulling every lever we could to increase fuel sovereignty and security in this country, and that's exactly what we're doing," he said.

David Crisafulli says the project will provide fuel security and improve "sovereign capability". (ABC News: Charlie McLean)

He said the project would help insure Australia against the effects of being at the end of a global supply chain.

"And that's why our focus is on making sure that right the way through the supply chain we do everything we can to increase our ability as a state and nation to drill, refine and store our own fuel."

Mr Crisafulli also repeated calls for the federal government to scrap a 2025 decision to ban fossil fuel projects from the national interest exemption under the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Act.

Qld premier says federal government should fast-track oil projects Queensland Premier David Crisafulli wants the federal government to fast-track environmental approvals for oil projects.

"Everyone we speak with tells us that the greatest impediment [to drilling] right now is that extra layer as part of the EPBC, which single-handedly added fossil fuels, and it happened late last year," he said.

"And we're asking the Commonwealth to remove that, and that would give the greatest signal to the market that these projects can occur."

Deputy Premier and minister for state development Jarrod Bleijie said the plan was similar to the Land Activation Program, intended to unlock land for housing.

"The Queensland government, the taxpayers, own vast amounts of land across the state and it has been utilised as it should be for years," he said.

" This is about partnering with the private sector, and we're serious about it. We're getting on with the job. "

Plan won't provide instant relief, opposition says

Opposition Leader Steven Miles said he welcomed government initiatives to improve the state's fuel security over the next decade, but criticised what he called "another David Crisafulli photo op".

"Queenslanders are struggling to fill their fuel tank right now. That's where David Crisafulli's focus should be," he said.

"[This plan] is similar to what we've been calling on them to do for seven or eight weeks now.

"But nothing in this photo op will help Queenslanders with their cost of living right now."

Deputy Opposition Leader Cameron Dick says the plan won't provide immediate cost-of-living relief to Queenslanders. (ABC News: Charlie McLean)

Deputy Opposition Leader Cameron Dick said it would be years before the plan saw fruition, and when it did, fuel needed to be kept for Queenslanders.

"Unless this oil is kept for Queenslanders, big oil companies will get the cream and Queenslanders will get the crumbs.

"David Crisafulli should … be using the billions of dollars in additional royalties that the government is receiving [due to the Iran war] to deliver direct cost of living relief to Queensland families today."

title: Aussies put Olympic heartbreak to bed with historic world title
text:
Australia's mixed team curlers Tahli Gill and Dean Hewitt have put the devastation of missing the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics behind them in the most emphatic way possible, beating Sweden’s Therese Westman and Robin Ahlberg to claim world championship gold in Switzerland.

The Aussies missed out on qualifying for the Olympics by the narrowest possible margin in December by losing in the semifinals of the qualification tournament in Canada.

However, December's heartbreak turned quickly into May delight after the pair recorded victory 8-4.

"It's amazing … it feels really good," Gill told World Curling.

"Oh gosh, I'm going to get emotional.

“It was really tough in December to just miss out on qualifying [for the Olympics].

"So we just came out here and had lots of fun. We just wanted to do our best, and we did, so we're really happy and proud.”

The Australian pair faced a tough battle but were always on top. (Supplied: World Curling/Ansis Ventins)

After swapping singles over the first two ends, the game exploded in the third, when Gill picked out two Swedish stones to score three and take a 4-1 lead.

Westman's pinpoint stone in a crowded house scored the Swedes two to reduce the deficit to 4-3 at the end of the fourth and the mid-session break.

"They took it to us, it was a tight game," Hewitt said.

"The first few ends we were just playing a little bit conservative while we learned the ice a bit.

"Then we tried to play a few more tougher shots to secure the bonus points."

Australia were in danger of losing points when they had the hammer immediately after the break, only for another superb stone from Gill stole a single, with the Swedish team failing to take advantage of a power play in the sixth, scoring just one point.

The Aussies made no such mistake on their powerplay though — they hadn't given up a steal when calling the bonus all week — and scored three to secure a famous victory.

Dean Hewitt and Tahli Gill wave to the small Australian contingent in the crowd. (Supplied: World Curling/Ansis Ventins)

"We got a little bit of a break in our powerplay to get three rather than two, but basically, we're just happy with staying with it every end the whole way through."

It is Australia's first ever world championship curling gold medal and comes just 12 months after the pair earned bronze in in Fredericton, Canada — an astonishing feat given Australia has no dedicated curling facility.

"Let that sink in," World Curling commentator Sander Rølvåg said.

"A nation without a dedicated curling club has just climbed to the very top of the world.

"It's not just a victory, it's a statement about what's possible if you really want it."

"Thank you to everyone back home," said Hewitt, who was also named MVP of the tournament following the semifinals.

"We couldn't have done it without you."

Why were Australia not at the 2026 Winter Olympics?

Heading into the 2026 Winter Olympics, Gill and Hewitt had risen to become the number one-ranked pair in the world.

That included a run whereby the pair won a bronze medal at the 2025 World Championships, Australia's first global curling medal.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

However, thanks to a disappointing 15th-place finish at the 2024 world titles, the Aussies did not have the necessary points to qualify automatically for the Olympics over the qualification period: they were ranked eighth with only the top seven, plus hosts Italy, guaranteed an Olympic spot.

The remaining two spots in the 10-team Olympic campaign were decided at a last-ditch qualification tournament in Kelowna, Canada where the Aussies came unstuck.

Despite wondering what might have been — Sweden won gold at the Olympic Games in Cortina, albeit with a different pairing of Isabella and Rasmus Wranå — the pair did not let the disappointment dominate them.

After the initial heartbreak of missing the Games wore off, Gill and Hewitt immediately turned their attention towards bouncing back at the World Championships, making vows at dinner the night of their defeat to come back stronger than ever.

Now, they are on top of the world.

title: Miami GP to start three hours early as storm fears grow
text:
The Miami Grand Prix has been moved forward by three hours to 3am Monday morning AEST because of the threat of storms.

The move came as world champion Lando Norris said drivers will be "thrown in at the deep end" by the expected wet weather.

The change to the start time comes after the previous two scheduled races, in Bahrain and Saudi Arabia, were cancelled due to the war in the Middle East.

A statement from F1 and the FIA said the decision was made because "the weather forecast [predicts] heavier rainstorms later in the afternoon, close to the original planned race start time".

"This decision has been taken to ensure the least amount of disruption to the race, and to ensure the maximum possible window to complete the grand prix in the best conditions and to prioritise the safety of drivers, fans, teams and staff," the statement read.

Norris is one of the vast majority of the grid who have not yet experienced the 2026 cars in wet conditions.

"It's going to be a big challenge on race day for everyone to perform, find the limit," Norris said.

"Obviously, you can't afford to make any mistakes. We're thrown in the deep end, but that's what we're here to do. I look forward to it."

McLaren dominates Miami sprint race McLaren finally halts the Mercedes dominance with a one-two finish in the Miami sprint race, before a difficult qualifying session.

The aim is to try to finish the race before the worst of the weather arrives, which is forecast to be about 5am AEST.

The heavy rain is expected to last for hours. Local laws in the US mean that the race could be suspended if there is a threat of a lightning strike at or around the circuit.

"It's obviously going to be a voyage into the unknown for everybody. When it rains here, it normally is pretty torrential, so it could be an interesting day," Australian driver Oscar Piastri said.

"It's just going to be what happens with the power-unit, how you get power, where you get power is in a computer's hands.

"Just making sure that that does roughly what we expect. Obviously, the margin for error when it's wet is significantly smaller."

Piastri is set to start seventh on the grid, after earlier finishing second behind Norris in the sprint race.

title: Dancing queens return to Trundle ABBA Festival to celebrate pop icons
text:
After a brief hiatus, one of regional Australia's most fabulously unexpected events is back, with the tiny central west NSW town of Trundle once again transformed into a sea of sequins, feather boas and pure pop joy.

For one day each year, the town of 335 people swaps tractors for tribute acts, grain trucks for glitter and everyday life for an all-out ABBA extravaganza.

"It's nowhere else in the world," coordinator Gary Crowley said.

"This is the only ABBA festival in the world and it's here in Trundle."

Annette and John Treloar travelled from Sydney to attend the festival. (ABC Western Plains: Catherine James)

Crowds travelled from across New South Wales and beyond, packing the main street for the return of the Trundle ABBA Festival, a celebration that began as an unlikely idea and grew into one of country Australia's most beloved events.

"We've had people from Western Australia, Darwin, Adelaide, Townsville, Melbourne come along," Mr Crowley said.

"It really draws people together and it's just so much fun."

Annette Treloar and her husband travelled from Sydney to attend.

"This has been on our bucket list for a while and we thought let's give it a go," Ms Treloar said.

"We've been to the Parkes Elvis Festival before but wanted to come to Trundle."

People travelled from interstate to attend the ABBA festival. (ABC Western Plains: Catherine James)

From quirky idea to country icon

The festival began in 2012, when residents took a chance on the odd concept of celebrating a Swedish supergroup in a wheatbelt town better known for silos and sunsets.

What followed was a regional success story few could have predicted.

"At our peak, we had around 6,000 people in attendance," Mr Crowley said.

Gary Crowley started the ABBA Festival in Trundle back in 2012. (ABC Western Plains: Catherine James)

But in recent years, the COVID pandemic, bad weather and money troubles meant the festival almost met its Waterloo.

Last year's event was cancelled when Parkes Shire Council ended its funding.

But it returned this year under new management, with a local community group answering the festival's SOS.

"A couple of people got in touch with me and said, 'What can we do? Can we do something?' and I said, 'You can have a festival, we'll just start again'," Mr Crowley said.

Dee and Craig Green have been married for more than 40 years and renewed their wedding vows on the day. (ABC Western Plains: Catherine James)

Dancing in the street

This year's program proved the festival, held on Saturday, was as playful as ever.

There was a world record attempt for the most people dancing to Dancing Queen in the middle of the main street, while nearby couples said "I do" all over again to the soundtrack of I Do, I Do, I Do, I Do, I Do.

"The vow renewal is a lot of fun," Mr Crowley said.

"It's a great chance to renew your wedding vows in front of 2,000 of your newest best friends."

Dee and Craig Green were one of 20 couples to renew their vows.

"We have been married for 41 years," Ms Green said.

Hundreds of fans rocked into Trundle for the ABBA Festival. (ABC Western Plains: Jen McCutcheon)

Economic benefits

The festival also delivers a major boost for local businesses.

Every year the event is held, it attracts visitors, boosts the economy and draws attention to a community that often has to work hard to stay visible.

"It's like a mini harvest for the town," Mr Crowley said.

"The showground is booked out and everyone packs into the pub after we finish."

title: Love story: When life changed in an instant, Lucy was Beau's rock
text:
Beau and Lucy Vernon were young, athletic and had big plans for the future. But when their lives were altered forever, these high school sweethearts built a strong partnership by supporting each other's dreams.

Whether it's romantic, platonic, heartbreaking or surprising, our Love Story series is about love in all its forms. Share yours with us.

These are Beau and Lucy's words.

'Shared love of sport' bonds young love

Beau: We were only 15 when we first met, we went to high school together at Phillip Island in Victoria.

Lucy was in the year above me.

We were down at the footy oval, kicking the footy with each other and one thing led to another and we started hanging out more.

It was a shared love of sport and being outdoors that brought us together.

Lucy's a very level-headed person, and she was just easy to get along with and have a good time with.

Beau and Lucy met as teenagers while playing sports at the same club in Victoria. (Supplied: Beau Vernon)

Lucy: As a teenager, I played netball, and Beau played footy at the same club.

And he came out of the change rooms one time without his shirt, and that was probably it for me.

Beau was always determined and driven, and he wanted to play AFL so I could see that he was someone who clearly had goals and worked hard. That was probably part of what drew me to him.

After high school, I wanted to pursue a psychology and marketing degree at a university in Melbourne.

Beau wanted to focus on AFL. It wasn't easy juggling the two, but it wasn't like either of us were happy to just go along with the other one.

We did have to do a lot of compromising.

'I knew something was very wrong'

Beau: We'd been together for eight years when the accident happened. I was 23, and Lucy was 24.

Lucy usually would come to my footy games, but that day she'd stayed in Melbourne.

It was down in Wonthaggi, so an hour and a half away. Lucy's parents were there watching the game, and I was also playing with my brother.

My teammate passed me the ball, and it was a bit too far out in front.

I bent down to pick up the ball, and someone running the other way hit me pretty much straight on, and my neck dislocated.

Beau spent months in the hospital after the on-field accident. (Supplied: Beau Vernon)

From that moment on the ground, I knew something was very wrong. I couldn't move my arms or my legs.

I was pretty scared.

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

Lucy: I was staying in Melbourne and doing some work for my master's degree, and I rang my sister-in-law who was at Beau's game to see how they were going.

She said, 'someone's down, it might be Beau, I'll let you know'.

But no-one knew how bad it was at that point.

Beau and Lucy Vernon say mutual support and respect have been the foundation of their relationship. (Supplied: Beau Vernon)

He got airlifted to Melbourne, and about eight emergency staff took us into a room. I thought 'this can't be good'.

They said, 'he's got a spinal cord injury', and you can't comprehend what that means at the time, I don't think your brain lets you.

I remember going into the intensive care unit where he was trying to be bubbly and then as soon as I left, I just couldn't talk to anybody.

Initially, you just have to think he's going to walk again.

The thought of him living like this was not something we could process for a fair few months.

'A celebration of how far we'd come'

Beau: In terms of my injury, it is C5/C6 quadriplegia or spinal cord injury. I've got no movement from my chest down.

I had to figure out how to be at home independently, without nurses or support workers.

Lucy was so good at letting me struggle and fail, which helped me a lot.

Getting through that really built a stronger bond together.

If you understand what I can do now, with what muscle function I've got, it's pretty amazing.

I remember Lucy's old man putting a bit of pressure on me to propose.

We'd been together for nine years and ended up buying an apartment in Melbourne.

I just laid a heap of flower petals down the hallway, and I put them in our car park and in the lift. Lucy was like, 'why are these here?', I think she kicked them out of the way.

She got into our apartment, and I was waiting at the end of the hall, and it was a good moment.

She's a very, very caring person, and I just love doing life with her. She's always been so supportive of me in what I want to do, including wheelchair rugby.

Beau Vernon with his daughter Layla, after winning bronze at the Paris Paralympic Games. (Supplied: Beau Vernon)

I represented Australia at the Paris Paralympic Games, and we won bronze. That was just unbelievable.

I'm a far better person now, having gone through what I've been through. I think I'm a better husband, I'm a better dad.

Lucy: We'd been to a few weddings after Beau's accident, and he got pretty upset at the thought of not being able to walk down the aisle.

He was still grieving everything.

So, I just thought we wouldn't get married, which didn't bother me.

The wedding was awesome because we had like 200 people in Beau's parents' backyard and everyone just wanted to celebrate after two years of pretty shitty times.

I think the wedding was probably when we felt we could start to set other goals.

We had to do IVF, so we were kind of like 'let's just start' in case we have issues.

Layla was born a year later, then in 2018 Joey was born, and in 2020 Ollie was born.

Our kids are the best part of our lives.

Beau and Lucy Vernon with their three children, Layla (left), Joey (right) and their youngest, Ollie. (Supplied: Beau Vernon)

It's definitely had a positive impact on the kids, having a dad with a disability.

They are a lot more empathetic and aware.

There's never been a conversation about, 'why me? Why did this happen?'

I never felt like Beau was trying to push me away, and he's always supported me.

I'm now doing my PhD, he plays wheelchair rugby at a high level, and I think that mutual support is really important.

title: Untold stories of women in whaling inspire art that delves into the past
text:
For decades, the whaling industry held Albany's economy and social fabric together.

When we look back on its history, the stories centre around the men who worked along the isolated and rugged south coast.

But what do the public know of the women who were also part of the industry?

From 1952 until its closure in 1978, families relocated to a station run by Cheynes Beach Whaling Company, about 70 kilometres east of Albany, to support its operations.

Albany local Barb Reader moved out in 1966 at the age of 18, and is now featured in a five-person portrait series honouring the women who helped shape its history.

Barb Reader's portrait is part of the Women in Whaling exhibition in Albany. (ABC Great Southern: Kate Forrester)

"I had just married, and had never left home before in my life," Ms Reader said.

"My husband at the time had got a job as paymaster and radio operator and was offered a house, but we had to be married — so we married."

Ms Reader said living at the station alongside other industry members felt like being part of one large, close-knit family, which helped the city thrive.

Albany was the home of Australia's last whaling station. (Supplied: History Great Southern - Kaartdijin Biddi Albany)

"The whaling station held Albany up, there were so many businesses that relied on us," Ms Reader said.

"It was a very strong industry here … when it closed, it felt like Albany sort of closed.

"My children had to move schools, and we had to move into town. So yes, it was quite hard."

Ms Reader said she could still remember the protests that took place in the lead-up to the closure of her home.

Barb Reader can recall watching the whaling boats return from around Bald Head. (Supplied: History Great Southern - Kaartdijin Biddi Albany)

"It got very political. I remember a big rally, people yelling and carrying on," she said.

"It became a greenie thing. I didn't understand it at the time."

Ms Reader said her husband and other families with members in the industry upon its closure were not paid any compensation.

"Places now close, people get compensation — but back then, there was nothing, there was no other job."

There are five portraits on display in the exhibit. (ABC Great Southern: Kate Forrester)

Women in Whaling

Nowadays, the whaling station is a museum, recording the history of Albany's whaling industry.

It's also home to the exhibit Ms Reader's story is part of.

Local artist Jo Wassell's project captures the histories of Albany's whaling women.

Wassell spent three months sitting with numerous women to hear their stories, also drawing on past photography and archival research to help shape her creations.

Artist Jo Wassell wanted to highlight the lived experience of the women at the whaling station. (ABC Great Southern: Kate Forrester)

"Traditionally, this is a male-dominated industry, so I was approached because the women's stories needed to be heard as well," Wassell said.

"It's lovely to dedicate a space to them and their lives beyond the whaling station because it's not just about whaling — it's also about showing how much richer their lives are because of it.

"I also want to point out that these women in my portraits are not just the wives of the men who worked here. Some of the women worked here, grew up here."

Wassell said she was interested in curating projects that shared untold stories.

" I'm particularly interested in stories of people who get overlooked, [and having] that one-on-one opportunity of hearing people's backgrounds. "

The Women in Whaling exhibition is showing at Albany's Historic Whaling Station until Sunday, May 31.

title: Toure scores again for Norwich in red hot preparation for World Cup
text:
Mohamed Toure has ended his whirlwind season at Norwich City with another goal to take him into double figures for the Championship club in just a dozen matches.

But while the red-hot striker can now start dreaming of a World Cup bow in North America, another Championship-based Socceroo, Riley McGree, still has unfinished business as he targets a Premier League place with Middlesbrough through the playoffs.

Toure finished up on the losing side for the Canaries in their final match of the season, as they went down 2-1 to Hull City, who, like Middlesbrough, booked their place in the four-team playoffs.

But the 22-year-old concluded his remarkable three-month campaign since he signed from Danish club Randers in familiar fashion, swivelling for a sharply taken eighth-minute opener with a neat volley from Anis Ben Slimane's brilliant lobbed assist.

It took him to 10 goals in his injury-interrupted spell, which also featured him becoming the first player in Norwich's 124-year history to score two hat-tricks in his first 10 matches for the club.

In other good news for Socceroos boss Tony Popovic, key midfielder McGree was back in action after another recent injury setback, coming on the second half of Middlesbrough's 2-2 draw at Wrexham.

McGree was part of Middlesborough's playoff campaign in 2023 when they also missed out on their dream of returning to the top-flight.

Now he'll be looking to fare better as they host Southampton in the playoff semis first leg next Saturday, with the return match at St Mary's on the following Tuesday. Hull play Millwall in the other semi, with the final set for Wembley on May 23.

The Socceroos may also be casting a few wistful glances at the form of Sydney's Adrian Segecic, who has been in scintillating form ever since he announced he was throwing in his lot with Croatia at international level rather than Australia.

The exciting 21-year-old midfielder ended his season with a fifth goal in three games, and his 11th of the season, for Portsmouth in their 1-1 draw with Birmingham City.

Segecic also kicked off a storm after Pompey had returned the ball following a stoppage for injury to one of their opponents, deciding to chase the ball down and firing just over the bar to leave Birmingham's players rounding on him, enraged at what they saw as unsportsmanlike conduct. He also got booked for his trouble.

title: North Queensland Games spark regional sport revival
text:
More than 2,000 competitors are travelling from across Queensland to compete in 30 sporting events at the North Queensland Games in Mackay.

During the next month, competitors will test their limits in sports including swimming, athletics, BMX and ocean canoeing.

The games' opening included lighting a torch and fireworks.

For some, like middle-distance runner Shailee Mewha, it is a relief to be competing close to home and avoiding costly trips to Brisbane in search of opportunities.

The North Queensland Games support entries from junior ages all the way to 35-plus masters. (ABC Tropical North: Bryn Wakefield)

"It's much easier just to have your own routine, sleep in your own bed, not travel as far," the 13-year-old said.

It is Mackay's first time hosting the month-long event in nearly a decade.

Aiming to increase regional participation, the Games are held in a new location every two years.

A torch has been lit to signify the beginning of the North Queensland Games. (ABC Tropical North: Bryn Wakefield)

Games spark club revival

One Mackay club has taken the games' arrival as a chance to rebuild a once-thriving local sport.

It has been close to eight years since Sunset Bay held an outrigger canoeing regatta.

Jacqui Fleming and Renee Martin are volunteering their weekend to record finish times and results in the regatta. (ABC Tropical North: Bryn Wakefield)

Volunteer Renee Martin said the club was slowly growing its membership and competitive experience after the COVID-19 pandemic, when participation slumped.

"To be able to host 140 paddlers here today, it shows support," she said.

"They've all come together to support this small club to host a regatta and to help out."

Outrigger teams have travelled from as far as Cairns to compete. (ABC Tropical North: Bryn Wakefield)

Yesterday, paddlers worked in teams of up to six, pulling through 20 kilometres of smooth, tropical ocean.

The canoes, which feature a lateral float, have a deep history in Hawaiian culture and a surprising local connection.

"The sport has grown, so it is Australia-wide now, outrigging," Ms Martin said.

"But it was mainly first developed around Airlie Beach, and the Whitsunday Islands were the ones to first commence [races]."

Olympian goes back to grassroots

Competitors at this year's North Queensland Games range from the under-5s age group all the way to Olympic athletes.

Heptathlete Tori West has been training for international events in Townsville. (ABC North Queensland: Baz Ruddick)

Olympian Tori West will be using the competition to tune up before travelling to Austria for a world-class heptathlon, a combination of seven track and field sports.

"This is an opportunity to go through competition rehearsal and treat it like a slightly more intense training session," she said.

West said she became an ambassador for the North Queensland Games to show junior athletes it was possible to overcome regional barriers with hard work.

"If people are focused enough and determined enough, things can happen," she said.

"It's not always just a resourcing issue."

Meanwhile, hundreds of athletes will be hitting the athletics track and swimming pools until Monday, with other competitions including basketball and disc golf running until the end of May.

title: Expert slams WA as last state to close child safety loophole
text:
People who fail Working with Children Checks interstate could still get one in WA because of what a leading child safety expert has described as a "shameful" delay in updating the state's laws.

Western Australia remains the only state or territory to have not passed legislation to mandate a 'banned in one, banned in all' approach to the checks.

Once in force, it would mean that a person denied a Working with Children Check (WWCC) anywhere else in the country would not be able to get one in WA.

Professor Higgins is also an Australian Catholic University professor. (RN Breakfast: Luke Siddham Dundon)

Institute of Child Protection Studies director Daryl Higgins said it was "one of the simplest things that we can do".

"It's I think a shameful act if governments sit by and don't take action when there's a very clear and easy way," he said.

WA left behind

In the wake of high-profile failures of child protection systems last year, attorneys-general from around the country agreed to six "priority actions", including better information sharing.

The first priority, a mutual recognition of failed WWCC tests, was meant to be done by "early 2026".

Attorneys-General from across the country are leading changes to Working with Children Check laws. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

Every other state has implemented those changes, and WA remains the only laggard after the ACT passed its legislation in December and the NT did the same in early February.

Broken Working with Children Check system Australia's Working with Children Check system has lulled people into a false sense of security and urgent reform is needed.

A WA government spokesperson said the WWCC reform was expedited but, after being introduced in December, it faced delays and is still awaiting debate in the upper house pending a committee report due in early May.

The spokesperson said it remains on track to meet national deadlines for consistent Working with Children Checks.

The WA government introducing changes to Parliament in December but they are yet to pass. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

While endorsing the checks as an important part of children protection systems, Professor Higgins said relying on them alone was "letting children down".

"We actually have to create cultures of safety, and so that involves training of people, it involves prevention strategies to be able to not only detect grooming when it might be occurring but to create safe environments so that it doesn't happen," he said.

Recent years have seen a number of high-profile failures of systems meant to keep children safe. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

WA's Commissioner for Children and Young People Jacqueline McGowan-Jones agreed.

"The biggest way that we can keep children safe is to have a culture in organisations of questioning, of listening to children's concerns," she said.

Jacqueline McGowan‑Jones says legislating child safe principles should be a "real priority". (ABC News: Keana Naughton)

National principles for child-safe organisations, created in the wake of the royal commission into institutional child sexual abuse, are meant to set out what that looks like.

In November, the Auditor General found WA was "lagging behind" with that also.

"The state's slow progress to implement the national principles is disappointing," Auditor General Caroline Spencer wrote, while acknowledging some organisations had voluntarily started to follow the principles.

"Especially given recent publicised failings in childcare that highlight the heartbreaking fact that children continue to experience harm."

The Auditor General was critical of WA's pace of reform. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

A government spokesperson said "significant work" was underway, including public awareness campaigns and capacity building in the sector.

Review underway into further changes

All of this is happening as the Department of Communities eyes further changes to the WWCC system.

An independent review, at a cost of nearly $200,000, is currently investigating "how applications from individuals with a criminal history are considered".

That includes looking at how charges of family and domestic violence and violence towards adults are considered in the process.

The Department of Communities is considering two sets of changes to Working with Children Checks. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

Also being considered is whether authorities should more readily issue temporary 'negative notices', which would stop people working with children, while further investigations take place.

A department spokesperson described it as a "proactive step".

"This process aims to ensure the WWCC Screening Unit is set up to continue prioritising child safety, as legislative reforms progress at both State and National level," they said.

Dr McGowan-Jones urged caution in any recalibration of that process, recalling a time when she was working in child protection that consideration was being given to a man working in shared accommodation.

"That person 20 years prior had had a family and domestic violence charge … and about 10 years prior a public urination charge," she said.

Dr McGowan-Jones says Working with Children Check policies need to be carefully calibrated. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

The Commissioner said the person was going to be denied a WWCC until it was reviewed because of how long had passed since the charges.

"And in fact, that person wasn't a risk and became one of the best staff members in that facility," she said.

" So we do have to be careful that we don't just assume a criminal history is severe enough or warrants them not working with children. "

The Department of Communities is also considering public feedback on other proposed changes, including what is classed as 'child-related work' and any exemptions to those categories.

title: Strong lead for Jo Palmer, close race in Huon in Upper House elections
text:
The Tasmanian government has claimed victory for incumbent MLC Jo Palmer after yesterday's Legislative Council election in her electorate of Rosevears, as a tight battle is shaping up between two independents in the southern seat of Huon.

Voters in two Legislative Council divisions — Rosevears, which takes in the West Tamar council area and some Launceston suburbs, and Huon, which includes the Kingborough and Huon Valley council areas — went to the polls yesterday.

Education Minister Jo Palmer has a comfortable lead in Rosevears, with 42.6 per cent of the first preference vote counted so far.

Jo Palmer has a strong lead in Rosevears. (ABC News: Kelsey Reid)

Trailing behind her is Labor candidate Ben McKinnon, with just under 25 per cent, followed by Greens candidate Charlene McLennan and Independent Susan Monson.

Premier Jeremy Rockliff today said it was clear Ms Palmer would be returned.

"Can I say to Jo and her family and her team, congratulations," Mr Rockliff said.

He said it was "a testament of a lot of hard work over the course of the last six years".

Ms Palmer said it was both "overwhelming" and "humbling" to see the results rolling in.

"It's quite scary when you put yourself out there and … you work as hard as you can, and then you just have to sit back on the night and keep hitting that refresh button and seeing the numbers coming in," she said.

"But it was a great relief and very humbling to be looking like I will be able to continue working for the next six years."

Jo Palmer with husband Andrew, sons Charlie and Alfie, dog Ruby and Premier Jeremy Rockliff, who says it's clear Ms Palmer will be returned as the member for Rosevears. (ABC News: Kelsey Reid)

Tasmanian election analyst Kevin Bonham said based on current numbers, it was "very likely" Ms Palmer would retain her seat.

However, he said the final result would most likely end up much closer than it currently looks.

"Most Legislative Council incumbents are returned and Labor is at a big disadvantage in the north of the state," Dr Bonham said.

"We'll have to wait and see what the final result is. It might not be all that lopsided after preferences … given the Green preferences will favour Labor strongly."

Tight race in Huon

In the southern seat of Huon, where incumbent Independent MLC Dean Harriss is vying for a second six-year term, it is a much tighter race, which Dr Bonham said was too close to call.

Mr Harriss currently holds the lead, having won 30.6 per cent of the vote so far.

Dean Harriss has a tense wait ahead of him as vote counting continues. (ABC News: Sophie Jaggers)

However, close behind is Kingborough Council Deputy Mayor Clare Glade-Wright, with 27.9 per cent.

Ms Glade-Wright is also running as an independent.

"I think Dean Harriss's lead, at this stage, is probably not enough," Dr Bonham said.

"We will have to wait and see when the vote is counted, but he really would have wanted a larger lead than that."

Clare Glade-Wright is in with a chance in Huon. (ABC News: Sophie Jaggers)

'Several stages' of counting to go through

TEC communications manager Glenn Jeffrey said the results would likely come down to preference distribution.

"We've completed the first stage of the count process, the first preference count on polling night, but there's still several stages to go through," Mr Jeffery said.

"It does look like we're going to have to go through the full distribution of preferences to see the full result and that takes a number of days.

"We will also have to wait probably for postal votes to return after that 10-day deadline."

Today, the commission will check and verify ballot papers, with counting to resume tomorrow.

Rechecks of first preferences will happen on Tuesday.

Voters in Huon and Rosevears went to the polls yesterday to elect their upper house representatives. (ABC News: Sophie Jaggers)

What were the issues on voters' minds?

Cost of living was top of mind for some who voted at the Huonville Primary School polling place yesterday.

"I'm sick of the cost of living, struggling to buy food and pay my mortgage," voter Heather said.

"I'm definitely hoping an independent will get in, someone that feels strongly about the Huon and loves the Huon," she said.

Cost of living was one of the main issues for Huon voter Heather. (ABC News)

As a nurse, Heather said she wants to see better healthcare for the region, especially for the elderly.

"It's a real struggle for them to get to hospital for specialists, that's a huge issue for me," she said.

Rosevears voter Julie said key issues for her are aged care and public transport.

"There's only so much money to go around so you have to focus on what's important for the whole community," she said.

Aged care and public transport were key issues for Rosevears voter Julie. (ABC News)

The Legislative Council is the upper house of the Tasmanian parliament.

Members are elected for six-year terms on a rotating basis, meaning that every year, two or three members are up for election.

It is currently dominated by independents who hold eight of the 15 seats.

The government and Labor each hold three, and the Greens one.

title: Backflip but no handshake as Ukrainian beats Russian at Madrid Open
text:
Marta Kostyuk of Ukraine beat Mirra Andreeva of Russia 7-5, 6-3 to win the Madrid Open title on Sunday morning AEST.

The two players, whose countries have been at war since Russia's invasion in 2022, did not shake hands at the net after the match or pose together at the awards ceremony.

The 23rd-ranked Kostyuk collapsed back onto the clay court and held her hands over her face after converting the match point when Andreeva's final shot flew long.

After getting to her feet, she did an acrobatic backflip while the crowd cheered.

Andreeva, meanwhile, slumped into her court-side seat and wept into a towel.

Mirra Andreeva cries into a towel after losing the Madrid Open women's final. (Getty Images: Oscar J. Barroso/Europa Press)

Kostyuk finished her victory speech with "glory to God and glory to Ukraine".

The win was Kostyuk's third WTA title, but her second in as many tournaments after she won at Rouen last weekend.

The eighth-ranked Andreeva, who turned 19 on Wednesday, congratulated Kostyuk when she spoke to the audience after being awarded her runner-up trophy.

"I would like to congratulate Marta and her team for the win today and for her season," Andreeva said, while Kostyuk clapped.

"You have won two tournaments in a row, so congrats."

Kostyuk beat the fifth-ranked Jessica Pegula and 13th-ranked Linda Noskova en route to the final, her first for a WTA 1000 tournament.

Against Andreeva, who was playing her third WTA 1000 final, Kostyuk converted all four of her break chances and saved four of six break chances for her opponent.

"It feels unbelievable to stand here right now," Kostyuk said.

"Up until last year I was 2-7 in Madrid and I never thought I would lift the title here. It was not my favourite tournament for sure, but thanks to the crowd for supporting me."

Top-ranked Jannik Sinner will face number three Alexander Zverev in the men's final on Monday morning at 1am AEST.

AP

title: A Liberal-Green government. Could it happen in Australia?
text:
In many ways, the Liberals and the Greens are complete opposites.

The two political parties sit on opposing sides of the political spectrum, and many of their key principles are in direct conflict.

At a federal level, the Liberals have described the Greens as "extreme", while the Greens have labelled the Liberals as "dangerous".

As you can tell, it's not the most straightforward pairing.

But, in the ACT, an Australian-first power-sharing arrangement between the two parties almost became a reality.

So, in politics, can opposites really attract?

'Can't always have everything you want'

Canberra Liberals leader Mark Parton says the idea of forming a coalition with the Greens was closer to happening than most people might expect. (ABC News: Monte Bovill)

In large parts of the country — including in South Australia, Victoria and Western Australia — Labor has been the recent dominating political force.

In the ACT, it's even more evident — Labor has governed the Australian capital for almost 25 years.

It's a sobering fact for the Canberra Liberals, who've suffered through seven straight election losses.

And, as current party leader Mark Parton told the ABC, being stuck on the opposition benches for that long can make you think of ideas that are "a little bit out there".

He says one idea — to form a coalition with the Greens and bring down Labor — was closer to happening earlier this year "than a lot of people think".

"It challenges many things in regard to how you expect my party and the Greens to operate," he said.

" You can't always have everything you want but sometimes you can achieve things if you are prepared to compromise. "

The two parties even went so far as to use the development of a local pool to test the power-sharing waters.

Outgoing ACT Greens leader Shane Rattenbury says the knee-jerk reaction that a Liberal-Greens coalition wouldn't work isn't necessarily right. (ABC News: Adam Shirley)

"Most people's honest reaction is this couldn't possibly work," then-Greens leader Shane Rattenbury said.

"But if you sit down and think about it, can you find a shared policy agenda that is about getting better outcomes, fixing issues like Phillip Pool, where we're seeing intransigence from the government to finding a good community solution there?"

Has it happened before?

There have been a few coalitions in Australia — the most notable being the long-standing partnership between the Liberals and the Nationals, both on the conservative side of politics.

On the progressive end of the spectrum, there have been deals between Labor and the Greens in Tasmania and the ACT — both use the Hare-Clark electoral system, where majority governments are harder to achieve.

An informal agreement between the Greens and the Liberals lasted for about two years on the island state back in the late 90s.

But if you look overseas, deals between left and right-wing parties are more common.

Professor Frank Bongiorno says coalitions between left and right-wing parties have occurred in Belgium, Finland and Germany. (Supplied: University of Canberra)

University of Canberra Professor Frank Bongiorno said many electoral systems overseas "induce a need for compromise".

"What often happens is you end up with a kind of rainbow coalition, in which you can have quite diverse political parties joining together," he said.

" Parties of the centre-right — the equivalent of our Liberal Party — have formed with the Greens in Belgium. It's happened in Finland, and it's happened in Germany as well. "

In New Zealand, coalition governments are the norm. Like in 2017, when Jacinda Ardern and Labour did a three-way deal with the Greens and right-wing party New Zealand First.

Years later, New Zealand First shifted sides, helping the conservative Nationals defeat Labour in 2023.

"We are not reinventing the wheel; it has been done in other places, and it can be done," Mr Parton said.

'The sky doesn't fall in'

Like any government, coalitions can have varying levels of stability, according to Professor Bongiorno.

"Some have been relatively stable, others not so much," he said.

"Everyone knows they are not going to get everything they want and electors get used to the proposition that what they thought they might be getting could be quite different from what they are going to get."

Professor Frank Bongiorno says some coalitions are more stable than others. (Supplied: University of Canberra)

But when opposing sides unite, they aren't inherently unworkable, as many might be led to believe.

"The sky doesn't fall in; they are able to implement policy," he said.

In fact, they can create opportunities.

"I am someone who likes to create opportunities to try and make things happen, to collaborate where possible and create things, and we sought to do that here," Mr Rattenbury said of the possible Liberal-Green coalition in the ACT.

A done deal?

So, with all this chat about bringing down Labor in the ACT, why didn't it happen?

Well, grassroots members of the Greens didn't back the idea.

"It was a robust discussion, no doubt about it, but certainly, at this point in time, there is no support for more formal arrangements with the Liberals in the current context," Mr Rattenbury said earlier this year.

And only a few months after the idea was shelved, Mr Rattenbury resigned, but denied the failed talks were a deciding factor.

Former Canberra Liberals leader Leanne Castley said she didn't think the deal would allow the party to remain true to its principles. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

Liberal members were also sceptical about the deal.

"I do not believe this deal is one that allows us to experience something different while remaining true to our principles," former Canberra Liberals leader Leanne Castley told the ACT Legislative Assembly.

"The only thing we have in politics is our principles; if we sacrifice those, what do we have left?"

But Mark Parton insists it was a conversation worth having and alluded to the fact that conversation might happen again.

"We'll see what the remainder of the term brings, and we will see what the landscape looks like after the election of 2028," he said.

A shift in thinking

The situation in the ACT is pretty unique, and it's hard to see the Liberals and the Greens in other states even contemplating doing a deal with each other.

But chatter about the potential agreement signals a shift in thinking — a shift towards more cooperation and a willingness to compromise in politics.

And as more Australian voters favour independents and minor parties, like One Nation, over Labor and the Liberals, we might see that shift happen elsewhere.

So, while it may seem hard to agree that 'opposites attract', they might just have to survive.

However, if the talk of the deal in the ACT is anything to go by, it's clear there's a lot of work to do — not only to convince people within their respective parties that it's a good idea, but the public at large.

title: Ryan Reynolds 'gutted' as Wrexham misses shot at EPL promotion
text:
Wrexham's dream of reaching the Premier League is over, for this season anyway.

And for Ryan Reynolds, it was tough to stomach.

"I am completely gutted by today's result but incredibly proud of our season," the movie star wrote on X after the Welsh club he co-owns missed out on a place in the playoffs in the second-tier Championship by drawing 2-2 with Middlesbrough in a dramatic final round of the regular season Sunday morning AEST.

The result allowed Hull to jump ahead of Wrexham and into sixth place — the fourth and final spot in the playoffs — courtesy of a 2-1 win over Norwich in a match played at the same time.

The winning goal for Hull, by Oli McBurnie in the 67th, appeared to be scored from an offside position but there are no video reviews in the English Football League.

It ended Wrexham's unprecedented run of three straight promotions under its famous owners, a streak that began by getting out of the fifth tier in the 2022-23 season and has been documented in the globally popular, Emmy Award-winning "Welcome to Wrexham" documentary series.

Still, seventh place marked the Wrexham's highest finish in its history, bettering the 15th position it achieved in the second tier in the 1978–79 season.

"We've come a long way in five years and this was the best result in our 150+ year history," Reynolds wrote alongside a graphic that showed how Wrexham has risen from the National League.

"More to do. But for now, we have so much to be proud of, Reds."

Elsewhere, Ipswich secured the second automatic promotion spot behind champion Coventry — and an immediate return to the Premier League — by beating Queens Park Rangers 3-0.

Ipswich is owned by US investment group Gamechanger 20 Limited and counts pop star Ed Sheeran as a minority shareholder.

Joining Hull in the playoffs, which begin next week and are over two legs, are Millwall, Southampton and Middlesbrough.

Millwall will face Hull, and Southampton will meet Middlesbrough.

It was a memorable campaign by Wrexham in its first season in the second tier since the 1980s.

However, the short-term pain was acute, and Wrexham's players sat on the ground and looked disconsolate after the final whistle, even though the Hull-Norwich match hadn't finished.

Wrexham started the day in sixth place, ahead of Hull on goal difference, and conceded in the fourth minute to Middlesbrough, only to score through Josh Windass and Sam Smith for a 2-1 lead by the 41st.

Middlesbrough hit back immediately with a 43rd-minute equaliser but Wrexham finished the stronger, squandering a string of great late chances for a winner that would have secured a playoff place on goal difference.

In the end, Wrexham finished two points behind Hull.

"This squad as it stands, with a preseason together, will be even stronger next year," said Wrexham manager Phil Parkinson, who oversaw a summer spend of about $US40 million ($55 million) last year.

"Of course we'll always look to add to that to try and give ourselves an even better chance … we know where we'd like to strengthen and what we need to improve on. We'll do that and we'll make this squad as strong as we possibly can to mount a challenge next year."

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.
